segments.text: ENDOPLASMIC RETICULUM

ENDOPLASMIC RETICULUM TYPES
Rough ER
ribosomes attached to the cytosolic side of the membrane
synthesis of secreted and membrane proteins
Smooth ER
no ribosomes
synthesis of lipids

ER ORGANIZATION
Interconnected membranous sacs and tubules of both rough and smooth ER

SECRETORY PATHWAY
Rough ER is first step in pathway
RER > Golgi > Vesicles > Cell exterior

PULSE-CHASE EXPERIMENTS DEFINED SECRETORY PATHWAY
George Palade et al. (late 1960s)
Pancreatic acinar cells
high level of secretion of digestive enzymes
distinct morphology
In vitro system with thin slices
Pulse: short incubation with 3H-leucine
Chase: add excess of “cold” (non-radioactive) leucine
Observe “hot” protein localization over time by electron microscopy

PULSE RESULTS
3 min pulse
Radioactivity (squiggles) at RER
Jamieson and Palade (1967) JCB 34: 597

CHASE RESULTS
7 min chase
Labeled proteins at Golgi

NEXT CHASE RESULTS
37 min chase
Labeled proteins in immature secretory vesicles (CV = condensing vacuole)

NEXT NEXT CHASE RESULTS
117 min chase
Labeled proteins in mature secretory vesicles or lumen (L)

STUDYING ROUGH ER FUNCTION
Microsomes
membrane bound fragments of smooth or rough ER
functionally active

COMPLETED PROTEINS ARE NOT TRANSLOCATED INTO THE RER
Co-translational translocation – translation and translocation occur at the same time

HOW ARE PROTEINS TARGETED TO RER?
“All” proteins start synthesis in cytosol
Elongating peptide exits through groove in ribosome (N-terminus first)
~ 40 aa not exposed to cytosol during synthesis

SECRETORY PROTEIN SIGNAL SEQUENCE
Signal sequence – hydrophobic rich 15-30 aa (at least 6 consecutive hydrophobic aa) at N-terminus
Signal recognition particle (SRP) binds signal sequence once exposed and pauses translation

CO-TRANSLATIONAL TRANSLOCATION
SRP binds SRP receptor (SR) on ER membrane
Both SRP and SR hydrolyze GTP
Ribosome “passed” to translocon
Translation resumes
SRP and SR released

CO-TRANSLATIONAL TRANSLOCATION
Signal peptidase cleaves (removes) signal sequence
Releases mature soluble protein into ER

HOW WAS CO-TRANSLATIONAL TRANSLOCATION MECHANISM DISCOVERED?
Gunter Blobel et al.
1970s-1980s
1999 Nobel Prize
Reconstitution experiments
disassemble process
reassemble with various combinations of components

SECRETED PROTEINS
Have longer preprotein version
Prolactin mRNA
codes for 25 kD protein (preprolactin)
but secreted protein = 22 kD (prolactin)

IN VITRO TRANSLATION SYSTEM
Contains amino acids, tRNAs, ribosomes, initiation factors, elongation factors, etc.
2 types
Reticulocyte lysate (RL): also contains SRP
Wheat germ extract (WGE): no SRP
Neither has rough ER

RECONSTITUTION COMPONENTS
Prolactin mRNA (PL mRNA)
WGE
Rough microsomes (RM)
Salt-stripped RM (K-RM)
Signal Recognition Particle (SRP)
Elastase-treated K-RM (EK-RM)
SRP receptor (SR)
Cytosolic domain of SR (cSR)

SIGNAL SEQUENCE CLEAVAGE
PL mRNA + WGE = 25 kD protein
PL mRNA + WGE + RM = 22 kD
Cleavage activity is RM associated

SIGNAL RECOGNITION PARTICLE
PL mRNA + WGE + K-RM = 25 kD
Salt stripping removes required peripheral protein
PL mRNA + WGE + K-RM + K-RM sup = 22 kD
Translocation reconstituted (YAY!)
Fractionate K-RM sup to purify SRP
PL mRNA + WGE + K-RM + SRP = 22 kD

SRP FUNCTION
PL mRNA + WGE + K-RM = 25 kD
PL mRNA + WGE + K-RM + SRP = 22 kD
PL mRNA + WGE + SRP = 8 kD
8 kD = signal sequence and sequence in ribosome
SRP pauses translation in cytosol

SRP RECEPTOR
PL mRNA + WGE + SRP + EK-RM = 8 kD
Proteolysis removes integral membrane protein that relieves SRP pause
PL mRNA + WGE + SRP + EK-RM sup = 25 kD
SRP pause relieved
Fractionate EK-RM sup to purify cSR
PL mRNA + WGE + SRP + cSR = 25 kD

SR CYTOSOLIC DOMAIN
PL mRNA + WGE + SRP + EK-RM + cSR = 22 kD
Translocation reconstituted (YAY AGAIN!)
RECONSTITUTION PRACTICE

TRANSMEMBRANE PROTEINS
Synthesized on rough ER
Contain signal sequences (like secreted proteins)
Co-translational translocation
Single pass and multipass
Different orientations

SINGLE PASS WITH N-TERMINAL SIGNAL SEQUENCE
Internal signal sequence = stop transfer

SINGLE PASS WITH INTERNAL SIGNAL SEQUENCE
Internal signal sequence = start transfer
Orientation depends on amino acid charges around signal sequence (+ on cytosolic side)

MULTIPASS TRANSMEMBRANE
Multiple start and stop transfer sequences
PREDICTING MULTIPASS ORGANIZATION

ER FUNCTIONS
Protein glycosylation
Protein folding
Formation of disulfide bonds
Proteolytic activation of proteins

PROTEIN GLYCOSYLATION
N-linked: added to asparagine
Oligosaccharide precursor: 14 sugars added at one time
Occurs in ER lumen

OLIGOSACCHARIDE TRANSFER
Oligosaccharyl transferase: enzyme that transfers sugar chain
Dolichol: lipid that sugar chain originally built on

ER PROTEIN FOLDING
Regulated by glycosylation state
Chaperones fold proteins (e.g., calnexin)

UNFOLDED PROTEIN RESPONSE IRE1 PATHWAY
BiP
